text: Hello, I am sharing a TME dissection for a low rectal cancer in a BMI-thirty-seven patient who is under five feet tall who got total neoadjuvant therapy for a T-four and two distal rectal cancer. I had a near complete response, could not even find a lesion, but MRI suggested still a T two and N one. So she was presented a tumor board and recommended for surgery.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection to isolate the superior hemorrhoidal vessel, with the ureter visible in the surgical field. A sponge is used to keep the small bowel out of the way, requiring frequent repositioning. The superior hemorrhoidal vein and artery are identified just distal to the takeoff of the left colic artery.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the tissue, focusing on the TME dissection. The nerves are swept posteriorly, maintaining a plane against the glistening yellow mesorectum. The surgeon works circumferentially, progressing down the right side and preparing to move anteriorly, taking care to avoid the vagina. The proximal black tattoo is visible a couple of centimeters above the lesion.

The surgeon performs a left lateral dissection to enter the left-sided TME plane, joining the previously dissected medial plane. The left ureter is identified and preserved as the dissection continues down the left side. The surgeon then transitions to anterior dissection, carefully avoiding the vagina while maintaining the integrity of the anterior mesorectum, ensuring the dissection plane is continuous circumferentially.

The surgeon navigates the dissection plane, avoiding excessive posterior movement towards the sacrum, using visible nerves as landmarks. The dissection progresses circumferentially, with improved retraction aiding anterior dissection. Arm number one is used to push against the vagina, maintaining the dissection plane. The surgeon searches for ICG tattoos, continuing distally due to limited visibility. Despite the patient's high BMI, the wide pelvis allows for clear visualization of the crackly dissection plane, particularly posteriorly and on the left side.

The surgeon continues anterior dissection, revealing one of the black tattoos, which is located one centimeter distal to the tumor. The ICG tattoo, placed distal to the black tattoo, is expected to come into view soon. The surgeon works within the rectovaginal septum, using blunt dissection to maintain the yellow fat with the rectum. The dissection progresses below the distal extent of the anterior mesorectum, approaching the rectum-vagina interface. Radiation fibrosis is noted, and the levator muscles are visible below the distal extent of the posterior mesorectum.

The surgeon continues circumferential dissection around the mesorectum, ensuring all tissue is taken with the specimen. An instrument maneuver reveals a tattoo on the patient's right side. As the dissection progresses lower, the surgeon switches to a vessel sealer for improved control and hemostasis, addressing small blood vessels in the area.

The surgeon identifies the right-sided tattoo and confirms that the mesorectum has been freed circumferentially, revealing the bare rectal wall. Switching to scissors, the surgeon dissects further distal onto the peritoneal body. The mesorectum is removed, and the surgeon prepares to staple beneath it, ensuring the mesorectum does not interfere with the stapler. The camera is repositioned to allow a vertical anterior to posterior stapling approach, while carefully avoiding the vagina. The surgeon confirms the position below the tattoo before firing the stapler.

The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, examining the pelvic cavity to assess the final surgical outcome. The procedure concludes with a coloanal anastomosis and the creation of a diverting loop ileostomy.
